# OpenScout

OpenScout is a local-first control plane for AI agents. It gives Claude Code,
Codex, and future harnesses one shared broker/runtime/protocol so agents can be
discovered, addressed, observed, and composed without every surface inventing a
separate state model.

Current posture: high-trust local developer pilots. Not enterprise-ready,
compliance-ready, or a hardened multi-tenant runtime. License posture is not yet
finalized; package manifests currently use `UNLICENSED`.

## Fast Context

- Runtime: `packages/runtime`
- Protocol: `packages/protocol`
- Web UI/server: `packages/web`
- Native macOS menu app: `apps/macos`
- Transitional desktop/CLI source: `apps/desktop`
- iOS: `apps/ios`
- Public CLI package: `packages/cli`
- Docs: `docs`

## Start Here

- [`README.md`](./README.md) — repo overview and bootstrap
- [`https://openscout.app/.well-known/scout.json`](https://openscout.app/.well-known/scout.json) — product manifest (remote)
- [`https://openscout.app/.well-known/agent.md`](https://openscout.app/.well-known/agent.md) — agent discovery entry (remote)
- [`docs/README.md`](./docs/README.md) — docs map and reading order
- [`install.md`](./install.md) — install/bootstrap guide with success criteria and support footprint
- [`docs/quickstart.md`](./docs/quickstart.md) — first healthy local run
- [`docs/current-posture.md`](./docs/current-posture.md) — maturity, trust, mesh, install footprint, and license boundaries
- [`docs/architecture.md`](./docs/architecture.md) — broker/runtime/protocol model (includes broker module map)
- [`docs/local-ports.md`](./docs/local-ports.md) — Scout-owned local TCP port range and exceptions
- [`docs/integrations.md`](./docs/integrations.md) — companion host package map
- [`docs/runtime-sessions.md`](./docs/runtime-sessions.md) — agent/session/endpoint semantics for harness lifecycle
- [`docs/scout-comms.md`](./docs/scout-comms.md) — client-facing comms workflows, records, routing rules, and contact-line grammar
- [`docs/mcp-api-posture.md`](./docs/mcp-api-posture.md) — core vs pro Scout MCP tool tiers
- [`docs/data-ownership.md`](./docs/data-ownership.md) — Scout-owned records vs observed harness material
- [`docs/agent-integration-contract.md`](./docs/agent-integration-contract.md) — minimum contract for agents and adapters
- [`docs/agent-identity.md`](./docs/agent-identity.md) — address grammar and resolution
- [`docs/collaboration-workflows-v1.md`](./docs/collaboration-workflows-v1.md) — question vs work item model
- [`docs/operator-attention-and-unblock.md`](./docs/operator-attention-and-unblock.md) — approvals, permissions, human-input notifications

- `llms-full.txt` — larger generated context bundle for copy/paste into agents
- [`docs/agent/INDEX.agent.md`](./docs/agent/INDEX.agent.md) — semantic spec index (dense subsystem models)
- [`docs/agent/README.agent.md`](./docs/agent/README.agent.md)

- [`docs/agent/scout-comms.agent.md`](./docs/agent/scout-comms.agent.md)
- [`docs/agent/runtime-sessions.agent.md`](./docs/agent/runtime-sessions.agent.md)
- [`docs/agent/pairing-runtime.agent.md`](./docs/agent/pairing-runtime.agent.md)
- [`docs/agent/scoutd.agent.md`](./docs/agent/scoutd.agent.md)
- [`docs/agent/current-posture.agent.md`](./docs/agent/current-posture.agent.md)
- [`docs/agent/integration-contract.agent.md`](./docs/agent/integration-contract.agent.md)

## Core Rules

- The broker is the canonical writer for Scout-owned coordination records.
- Scout owns messages, invocations, flights, deliveries, bindings, agent registrations, questions, and work items created through Scout.
- Scout observes external harness transcripts such as Claude Code and Codex JSONL; do not bulk-import those turns as first-party Scout messages.
- Mesh means reachability and coordination, not exactly-once delivery, global consensus, or transcript replication.
- First-run order: `scout --help`, `scout setup`, `scout doctor`, `scout whoami`, `scout who`, then `scout send --to <agent-from-scout-who> ...`.
- Use `scout send` / `messages_send` for durable messages and update receipts.
- Use `ask` through MCP, or `scout ask` through the CLI, for agent-to-agent
  work. `ask` is the creation API; invocation and flight records are side
  effects observed with `invocations_get` and `invocations_wait`.
- If you do not have a specific agent/session in mind, pass `projectPath` to
  MCP `ask` or use `scout ask --project <path>`; add `harness` / `--harness`
  when the capability matters. The broker resolves or creates the concrete
  worker for that project, so agents should not guess generic names like
  `claude.main` first.
- Agent cards, labels, and exact agent ids are fresh-session targets for new
  work; use `targetSessionId` / `session:<id>` only to continue exact prior
  harness context.
- Follow-up should use the durable broker handle returned by the ask (`ref`,
  `flightId`, `conversationId`, `workId`, or `session:<id>`). Promote a worker
  to a memorable name only after Scout has routed a known-good worker, ideally
  using a broker-suggested handle.
- Treat the base agent identity as the vanilla project/workspace identity.
  Harness, model, profile, node, and session details are instance constraints
  layered onto that identity only when the caller needs them.
- Use session semantics for harness lifecycle: cards are identities, sessions are concrete Claude/Codex runtimes, endpoints attach agents to sessions.
- Use explicit target fields; do not hide routing in message body text.
- If a host-side approval prompt is stuck, use the host UI or a host integration
  that forwards it into Scout; an MCP server cannot see prompts intercepted
  before the Scout tool call.
- In Scout-aware composers, `>> target` is the preferred typed route operator;
  strip it into structured routing metadata before sending payload to the broker.

## Verification Commands

```bash
bun run --cwd apps/desktop check
npm --prefix packages/runtime run check
npm --prefix packages/protocol run check
```

Root `npm run check` may fail if a workspace uses a bare `tsc` binary that is
not installed in the current shell environment. Prefer package-local checks when
working in a specific area.
